section.title: Warning Against False Teachers
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with its reason for being. Paul greets Timothy, then states the charge he left him at Ephesus: stop certain men from teaching a different doctrine. The false teaching is contrasted with the true goal of instruction, love from a pure heart, and with the proper, lawful use of the law, which agrees with the glorious gospel.
section.thesis: The church must guard sound doctrine, because false teaching breeds speculation and empty talk, while the aim of true instruction is love from a pure heart, a clear conscience, and a sincere faith, all in step with the glorious gospel of the blessed God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the apostolic greeting to Timothy; the charge to silence those teaching a different doctrine; and the lawful use of the law over against those who misuse it.

units.label: Greeting to Timothy
units.note: apostle by the command of God

verses.text: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the command of God our Savior and of Christ Jesus our hope,
To Timothy, my true child in the faith: Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

units.label: The charge against false teachers
units.note: the goal of instruction is love

verses.text: As I urged you on my departure to Macedonia, you should stay on at Ephesus to instruct certain men not to {{heterodidaskaleo|teach false doctrines}}
or devote themselves to {{mythos|myths}} and endless genealogies, which promote speculation rather than the {{oikonomia|stewardship}} of God’s work, which is by faith.
The goal of our instruction is the {{agape|love}} that comes from a pure heart, a clear {{syneidesis|conscience}}, and a sincere faith.
Some have strayed from these ways and turned aside to empty talk.
They want to be teachers of the law, but they do not understand what they are saying or that which they so confidently assert.

units.label: The lawful use of the law
units.note: in step with the glorious gospel

verses.text: Now we know that the {{nomos|law}} is good, if one uses it legitimately.
We realize that law is not enacted for the righteous, but for the lawless and rebellious, for the ungodly and sinful, for the unholy and profane, for killers of father or mother, for murderers,
for the sexually immoral, for homosexuals, for slave traders and liars and perjurers, and for anyone else who is averse to {{hygiaino|sound}} teaching
that agrees with the glorious {{euangelion|gospel}} of the blessed God, with which I have been entrusted.

heterodidaskaleo.gloss: To teach a different or other doctrine, from heteros, other, and didaskalos, teacher. It names teaching that departs from the apostolic gospel. The very charge of the letter is to stop such teaching at Ephesus; sound doctrine and different doctrine are the two poles the whole book moves between.

mythos.gloss: A myth, fable, or made-up tale, opposed to truth. Paul repeatedly warns Timothy against myths, here paired with endless genealogies. Such speculation feeds curiosity and pride but not faith, standing in contrast to the stewardship of God's work.

oikonomia.gloss: Household management, stewardship, or God's ordered plan. The gospel advances God's stewardship, His saving administration, received by faith, not the endless speculation the false teachers prize. Sound teaching serves God's work; myths only distract from it.

agape.gloss: Self-giving love, the highest New Testament word for love. Paul names it as the goal of all true instruction: not mere knowledge or debate, but love flowing from a pure heart, a clear conscience, and sincere faith. Doctrine that does not lead to love has missed its aim.

syneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner witness that judges right and wrong. A clear conscience is one of the three springs of genuine love. The false teachers, by contrast, have shipwrecked faith and conscience together; a defiled conscience and false doctrine travel as companions.

nomos.gloss: The law, especially the law of Moses. Paul affirms the law is good when used lawfully: it exposes sin and restrains the lawless, driving sinners to the gospel. The would-be teachers of the law misuse it, not grasping what they so confidently assert.

hygiaino.gloss: To be healthy or sound, the root of our word hygiene. A signature word of the Pastoral Epistles for doctrine that is spiritually wholesome and health-giving. Sound teaching agrees with the gospel; whatever is contrary to it is, by implication, diseased.

euangelion.gloss: Gospel, good news. Paul calls it the glorious gospel of the blessed God, entrusted to him as a stewardship. It is the measuring line for all teaching: sound doctrine agrees with the gospel, and the law itself, rightly used, points toward it.

basic.context: Paul greets Timothy as his true child in the faith and reminds him why he was left at Ephesus: to stop certain men from teaching a different doctrine full of myths and endless genealogies. That teaching produces speculation and empty talk; the true aim of instruction is love from a pure heart. Paul then shows that the law is good when used lawfully, to expose sin, and that it agrees with the gospel entrusted to him.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul is charging a younger pastor to protect a church from error. The passage is prescriptive about guarding sound doctrine and about the goal of teaching being love, not just knowledge. The list of sins in verses 9 and 10 shows the law's proper use, exposing sin, not a checklist to condemn without hope, since the gospel that follows is for exactly such sinners.
basic.originalLanguages: The false teachers heterodidaskaleo, teach a different doctrine, chasing mythos, myths, instead of God's oikonomia, stewardship. The goal of true instruction is agape, love, from a pure heart and a clear syneidesis, conscience. The nomos, law, is good when used lawfully, and sound, hygiaino, teaching agrees with the euangelion, the glorious gospel of the blessed God.
basic.typology: The law that exposes the lawless is a schoolmaster pointing to Christ, in whom alone the sinner is made righteous. Paul as a steward entrusted with the gospel foreshadows every faithful minister who guards the deposit of truth handed down.
basic.christ: The greeting names Christ Jesus our hope, and the whole charge is anchored in the glorious gospel of the blessed God. The law rightly used drives sinners to Christ, and sound teaching is measured by whether it agrees with the good news of what God has done in Him.
basic.god: God is called our Savior already in verse 1, and the gospel is the gospel of the blessed God. He is the source of grace, mercy, and peace, and the owner of the stewardship the gospel advances. He entrusts His truth to servants and calls them to guard it faithfully.
basic.crossReferences: Titus 1 gives the same charge to silence false teachers. Galatians 3 shows the law as a guardian leading us to Christ. Second Timothy 1 speaks of guarding the good deposit entrusted to us.
basic.oneStory: From the start God has spoken truly and been opposed by lies and counterfeits. Here He raises up guardians of the gospel to keep His people anchored to the truth. The law exposes sin, the gospel saves sinners, and the whole letter serves God's long work of building a household of faith around His Son.

context.literary: The letter opens with a compact greeting, then moves straight to its occasion in verse 3: the charge to stop false teaching. Verses 5 to 7 contrast the goal of instruction with those who have strayed, and verses 8 to 11 defend the lawful use of the law, ending on the gospel entrusted to Paul, which sets up his testimony in the next movement.
context.historical: Timothy was left at Ephesus, a major city with a strong church, now troubled by teachers fascinated with myths, genealogies, and speculative use of the law. Such teaching may have blended Jewish legal ideas with early proto-gnostic speculation, promoting endless debate rather than godliness.
context.canonical: This opening states the theme of the Pastoral Epistles: guarding sound doctrine and ordering the household of God. It stands with Titus and 2 Timothy as Paul's charge to the next generation of leaders to keep the church anchored to the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To remind Timothy of his commission at Ephesus, to arm him against false teaching, and to set the true goal of instruction, love from a pure heart, over against speculation, so the church holds to the gospel entrusted to Paul.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The charge to guard doctrine and to aim at love is prescriptive for the church in every age. The specific errors, myths and genealogies, describe the Ephesian situation, while the underlying principle, reject teaching that departs from the gospel, endures. The vice list illustrates the law's exposing work, not a licence to condemn without offering the gospel.

debates.question: What were the myths and endless genealogies?

views.label: Jewish legends and legal speculation
views.case: The teachers wanted to be teachers of the law, verse 7, so many read the myths as Jewish legendary expansions and genealogical speculation tied to misuse of the Mosaic law.

views.label: Early proto-gnostic speculation
views.case: The later warning against so-called knowledge in 6:20 leads some to see an incipient gnostic strand, with cosmic genealogies and ascetic tendencies, seen in chapter 4.

debates.bearing: Either way the fault is the same: speculation that feeds pride and division instead of the faith-shaped stewardship of God, so the pastoral remedy is unchanged.

debates.question: What is the lawful use of the law in verses 8 to 11?

views.label: To expose and restrain sin
views.case: The law is aimed at the lawless, laying bare their sin, so that it drives them toward the gospel rather than serving as a ladder of self-righteousness.

views.label: A rebuke of the false teachers' misuse
views.case: The point is chiefly that the would-be teachers of the law handle it wrongly; used legitimately it agrees with the gospel, exposing sinners like those in the list who need saving.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the law is good but must be used as God intends, to reveal sin and point to Christ, not as fuel for speculation or pride.

study.originalLanguages: The problem is stated with heterodidaskaleo, to teach a different doctrine, a coined word for departing from the apostolic message. Its content is mythos, myths, and endless genealogies, which breed speculation rather than serving God's oikonomia, His ordered saving stewardship received by faith. Against this, the goal of instruction is agape, self-giving love, springing from a pure heart, a clear syneidesis, conscience, and a sincere faith. The nomos, the law, is good when used lawfully: it is aimed at the lawless to expose their sin. Whatever teaching is sound, hygiaino, healthy and health-giving, agrees with the euangelion, the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which Paul holds as a trust.

typology.fulfillment: A guardian that shuts sinners up to grace and leads them to Christ, Galatians 3:24, in whom alone they are justified.

typology.type: Paul entrusted with the gospel as a steward
typology.fulfillment: Points to every faithful minister who guards the good deposit, 2 Timothy 1:14, under Christ the Lord of the household.

study.christ: The greeting already fixes our eyes on Christ Jesus our hope, and the movement ends on the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which is the good news of what God has done in Him. The law, rightly used, is not opposed to this gospel but agrees with it: by exposing sinners as lawless and ungodly, it drives them to the Savior. Timothy's whole task at Ephesus is to keep the church fastened to Christ and His truth against every different doctrine.
study.god: God is named our Savior in the first verse and the blessed God whose gospel is glorious in the last. He is the giver of grace, mercy, and peace, and the Master whose stewardship the gospel advances. He entrusts His truth to servants and holds them responsible to guard it, refusing to let His people be led astray by speculation dressed up as teaching.

crossReferences.note: A parallel charge to silence those who teach for gain and give heed to Jewish myths.
The law as a guardian that reveals sin and leads us to Christ, its lawful use.
Hold the pattern of sound words and guard the good deposit entrusted to you.
The same test applied again: teaching must agree with the sound words of our Lord Jesus Christ.

study.oneStory: God has always spoken truth and always been shadowed by counterfeits and lies. Here He stations Timothy at Ephesus to guard the gospel so His people are not swept away by speculation. The law exposes sin and the gospel saves sinners, and this opening charge belongs to God's long work of gathering and protecting a household of faith around His Son, Christ Jesus our hope.

quiz.q: Why did Paul leave Timothy at Ephesus?

options.text: To instruct certain men not to teach a different doctrine.
options.note: This is Paul's stated charge in verses 3 and 4.

options.text: To raise money for the Jerusalem church.
options.note: A real Pauline concern elsewhere, but not the reason given here; the issue is false teaching.

options.text: To plant a brand new church in the city.
options.note: The Ephesian church already existed; Timothy was to guard and order it.

options.text: To wait quietly until Paul returned.
options.note: He was left for active, demanding work of correction, not idle waiting.

quiz.q: What is the goal of true instruction, according to Paul?

options.text: Love that comes from a pure heart, a clear conscience, and a sincere faith.
options.note: Verse 5 names love as the aim, springing from these three.

options.text: Winning every argument with the false teachers.
options.note: Error must be corrected, but the aim is love, not victory in debate.

options.text: Mastering myths and genealogies more thoroughly than others.
options.note: These are exactly what Paul warns against as mere speculation.

options.text: Keeping the deepest teaching hidden from ordinary believers.
options.note: Nothing here hides truth; the goal is love built on sincere faith.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about the law?

options.text: The law is good, if one uses it lawfully, to expose the lawless.
options.note: Verses 8 and 9 affirm the law is good when used as intended.

options.text: The law is bad and should be discarded by Christians.
options.note: Paul says the opposite; the fault is misuse, not the law itself.

options.text: The law saves the righteous who keep it well.
options.note: The law exposes sin; it is not a ladder of self-righteousness.

options.text: The law has nothing to do with the gospel.
options.note: Sound teaching, and the lawful use of the law, agree with the gospel.

quiz.q: What test does Paul give for sound teaching?

options.text: It agrees with the glorious gospel of the blessed God.
options.note: Verse 11 makes the gospel the measure of sound doctrine.

options.text: It sounds impressive and confident.
options.note: The false teachers were confident, verse 7, yet did not understand what they said.

options.text: It is popular with the crowd.
options.note: Popularity is no gauge of truth; agreement with the gospel is.

options.text: It offers new speculation beyond Scripture.
options.note: Speculation is the error; sound teaching agrees with the gospel.

quiz.q: What went wrong with those who strayed from the goal of instruction?

options.text: They turned aside to empty talk, wanting to teach what they did not understand.
options.note: Verses 6 and 7 describe empty talk and confident ignorance.

options.text: They simply preferred a quieter style of worship.
options.note: The problem was false doctrine and empty talk, not style.

options.text: They cared too much about love and not enough about rules.
options.note: The reverse; they missed love, the goal, and chased speculation.

options.text: They taught the plain gospel too boldly.
options.note: They departed from sound teaching; the gospel was not their problem.

questions: Paul says the goal of all instruction is love from a pure heart. When you learn or teach the Bible, is love the aim, or can knowledge become an end in itself?
The false teachers were confident but did not understand what they were saying. Where might confidence be outrunning real understanding in your own speech about God?
A clear conscience is named as one spring of genuine love. Is there anything troubling your conscience that you need to bring honestly to God?
The law is good when used lawfully, to expose sin and drive us to Christ. How has seeing your own sin more clearly deepened your gratitude for the gospel?
Paul was entrusted with the gospel as a stewardship. What has God entrusted to you that you are responsible to guard and pass on faithfully?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote gives the literal phrase rather than the stewardship of God in faith, underlining that oikonomia means God's ordered saving plan received by faith.
For slave traders carries a footnote reading or for kidnappers; the term denotes those who seize and sell human beings.
Sound teaching translates a word meaning healthy or wholesome, a recurring image in the Pastoral Epistles for doctrine that gives spiritual health.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 1:1-11 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
